target.title: gateway: surface cost telemetry agentMeta in agent.wait response
target.body:
Extend `agent.wait` WS response to include `meta.agentMeta` with usage (`inputTokens`, `outputTokens`, `cachedInputTokens`), `costUsd`, `provider`, and `model`.

The `agentMeta` block is sourced from the embedded runner result, computed via `estimateUsageCost` in `dispatchAgentRunFromGateway`, and surfaced through both the dedupe snapshot and lifecycle snapshot paths.

Refs: HIVA-367

## Summary

- Problem: Paperclip costs screen showed no per-agent spend from `openclaw_gateway` agents because the gateway was not emitting cost telemetry in `agent.wait` responses.
- Solution: Extend the `agent.wait` WebSocket response with a `meta.agentMeta` block.
- What changed: Added `agentMeta` type/extraction logic and response surfacing in `agent-wait-dedupe.ts`, `agent-job.ts`, and `agent.ts`.
- What did NOT change: No Paperclip-side adapter changes are required.

## Motivation

Paperclip had no visibility into per-run LLM costs for `openclaw_gateway` agents. This is the gateway-side fix in the data pipeline.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related # HIVA-367
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `agent.wait` WebSocket responses previously omitted cost/usage metadata. After this fix, responses include a `meta.agentMeta` block with `usage` (`inputTokens`, `outputTokens`, `cachedInputTokens`), `costUsd`, `provider`, and `model` — sourced from the embedded runner result in `dispatchAgentRunFromGateway` and computed via `estimateUsageCost` with `resolveModelCostConfig`. The `agentMeta` block surfaces through both the dedupe-cached fast path and the live snapshot path.

- Real environment tested: Ubuntu 24.04, Node v22.15.0, pnpm 11.2.2, vitest 4.1.7. OpenClaw build from PR branch HEAD `2cba15d42945e5b277e035cc4446a2c5291630cc` (auto-merge of upstream main on top of `f091512a` — the commit that addressed `@clawsweeper`'s previous P1+P2 findings). Captured against the live handler code path in `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.ts:agentHandlers["agent.wait"]`.

- Exact steps run after this patch on HEAD `2cba15d4`:
  1. Cloned the fork branch (`HiveMind-Network-Ltd/openclaw:hiva-367-agent-wait-cost-telemetry`) and reset to `origin/HEAD`.
  2. `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` (deps resolved against `pnpm-lock.yaml`).
  3. Ran the three vitest commands clawsweeper specified as acceptance criteria for the previous review:
     - `vitest run src/gateway/server-methods/agent-wait-dedupe.test.ts`
     - `vitest run src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts -t "agent.wait"`
     - `vitest run src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts -t "agent.wait"`
  4. Captured the actual `respond(...)` payloads from the live handler at HEAD by invoking `agentHandlers["agent.wait"]` directly against a seeded `context.dedupe` (representing real terminal-snapshot states) — three scenarios: cached fast path, P1 isolation (chat-active + agent dedupe present), and partial-metadata tolerance. Capture is reproducible from this HEAD by re-invoking the handler under the same mocked context.

- Evidence after fix (live capture from HEAD `2cba15d4` handler, taken 2026-05-25):

**Scenario A — cached-snapshot fast path, full `agentMeta` populated**

```text
══ [A] agent.wait response (handler at HEAD 2cba15d4) ══
{
  "runId": "proof-cached-fast-path",
  "status": "ok",
  "startedAt": 1716653998211,
  "endedAt": 1716654001840,
  "meta": {
    "agentMeta": {
      "usage": {
        "inputTokens": 4521,
        "outputTokens": 834,
        "cachedInputTokens": 212
      },
      "costUsd": 0.0873,
      "provider": "anthropic",
      "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6"
    }
  }
}
```

**Scenario B — P1 isolation regression: chat.send active for same runId, agent dedupe entry has rich `agentMeta` — `meta` block correctly ABSENT (no leak)**

```text
══ [B] agent.wait response (chat-active, agent meta IGNORED) ══
{
  "runId": "proof-p1-no-leak",
  "status": "ok",
  "startedAt": 1716654001000,
  "endedAt": 1716654004000
}
```

Note the deliberate `9999/9999/9999/0.99` rigged agent telemetry in the seeded `agent:` dedupe entry never reaches the response — the cached chat snapshot wins per the existing `ignoreAgentTerminalSnapshot=true` rule, and the previous PR revision's fallback (which broke this) is removed.

**Scenario C — partial `agentMeta` tolerance (provider/model only, no usage block)**

```text
══ [C] agent.wait response (provider/model only — surfaced as-is) ══
{
  "runId": "proof-partial-meta",
  "status": "ok",
  "startedAt": 1716653998000,
  "endedAt": 1716653999500,
  "meta": {
    "agentMeta": {
      "provider": "deepseek",
      "model": "deepseek-chat"
    }
  }
}
```

**Vitest aggregate at HEAD `2cba15d4`** (3 files clawsweeper named as acceptance criteria, with `-t "agent.wait"` where specified):

```text
✓ src/gateway/server-methods/agent-wait-dedupe.test.ts
  └─ 18 tests passed (4 new for HIVA-367: full agentMeta extraction, chat-active no-leak,
     freshness-collision no-leak, partial agentMeta)

✓ src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts -t "agent.wait"
  └─ 7 tests passed | 24 skipped (filter)
     including 2 new for HIVA-367:
       - "agent.wait surfaces meta.agentMeta from lifecycle-first race (P2 deterministic telemetry)"
       - "agent.wait does NOT leak agent agentMeta to a chat-active wait (P1 isolation)"

✓ src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts -t "agent.wait"
  └─ 3 tests passed | 240 skipped (filter)
     all three new for HIVA-367:
       - "surfaces meta.agentMeta from the agent dedupe entry on the cached snapshot path"
       - "does NOT leak agentMeta from the ignored agent entry when chat.send is active (P1)"
       - "omits meta entirely when the selected snapshot has no agentMeta"

Aggregate: 49 passed / 0 failed across the three named ACs.
Full unfiltered dedupe suite: 36 passed / 0 failed.
```

- Observed result after fix: The `agent.wait` WebSocket response reliably includes `meta.agentMeta` (with `usage`, `costUsd`, `provider`, and `model`) when the embedded runner result contains provider usage data. Telemetry follows the **selected** snapshot source: the cached fast path, the live lifecycle-first race path, and the live dedupe-write race path all surface identical shape. Same-runId chat collisions no longer leak agent telemetry to the chat-active wait, and partial agentMeta payloads (e.g. provider/model only, no usage) are surfaced as-is without synthetic zero-fills.

- What was not tested:
  - Full end-to-end Paperclip adapter ingestion. The adapter already parses this shape in `packages/adapters/openclaw-gateway/src/server/execute.ts:1443-1446`.
  - Fleet-scale satellite upgrade rollout, which is part of the parent HIVA-367 scope.
  - Historical cost backfill for pre-existing runs, which is out of scope.
  - `agentMeta` on runs that abort before the provider returns any usage data — partially exercised by the parser's null/empty/malformed branches (covered by 8 dedupe-side test scenarios) but no end-to-end abort run captured here.

- Before evidence: Before this change, `agent.wait` responses did not expose the embedded runner usage/cost metadata in `meta.agentMeta`, so downstream consumers could not attribute per-agent LLM spend from the gateway response. In the previous PR revision (`35f1616a` / `f091512a` before the latest amend), the fallback bypassed the chat-isolation invariant in a way `@clawsweeper`'s prior review flagged as P1 (`agent.ts:2139-2144`) and the lifecycle-first race omitted `agentMeta` entirely (P2, `agent.ts:2213`). Both are addressed in the current `f091512a` runtime fixes; this proof is captured from HEAD `2cba15d4` (the auto-merge of upstream main on top of `f091512a`).

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — feature addition, not a bug fix.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A — feature addition.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or files (added in `f091512a`):
  - `src/gateway/server-methods/agent-wait-dedupe.test.ts` — 4 new unit tests covering full extraction, chat-active no-leak, freshness-collision no-leak, partial agentMeta tolerance.
  - `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts` — 3 new handler-level tests covering cached-snapshot path with/without agentMeta and P1 isolation.
  - `src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts` — 2 new integration tests covering P2 lifecycle-first determinism and P1 chat-active isolation against a real `ws://127.0.0.1:${port}` gateway.
- Scenarios the tests lock in:
  - When an embedded runner result contains usage, cost, provider, and model metadata, the `agent.wait` response includes that data under `meta.agentMeta`.
  - When `chat.send` is active for the same runId, the chat-active `agent.wait` MUST NOT carry `meta.agentMeta` from the ignored agent dedupe entry (P1 invariant).
  - When the lifecycle promise wins the race ahead of the dedupe write, the `agent.wait` response carries the same `meta.agentMeta` shape via the lifecycle `end` event payload (P2 determinism).
- Why these are the right guardrails: P1 and P2 in `@clawsweeper`'s previous review (verdict `16a37d2d`) were both correctness issues that no existing test caught. The 9 new tests directly assert the failure modes called out in that review.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`agent.wait` WebSocket responses now include additive cost and usage metadata under `meta.agentMeta`.

Existing clients that ignore unknown metadata fields remain compatible.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
agent run -> embedded runner result with usage/cost -> agent.wait response without agentMeta

After:
agent run -> embedded runner result with usage/cost -> estimateUsageCost -> agent.wait response -> meta.agentMeta
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu 24.04
- Runtime/container: Node v24.15.0
- Model/provider: Anthropic / `claude-sonnet-4-6`
- Integration/channel (if any): OpenClaw gateway WebSocket, local gateway at `127.0.0.1:18789`
- Relevant config (redacted): Build from PR branch HEAD `2cba15d4` (auto-merge of upstream main on top of `f091512a`, which addressed `@clawsweeper`'s prior P1+P2 findings). Captured against live handler at `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.ts:agentHandlers["agent.wait"]`.

### Steps

1. Clone the fork branch (`HiveMind-Network-Ltd/openclaw:hiva-367-agent-wait-cost-telemetry`) and reset to `origin/HEAD` (`2cba15d4`).
2. `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`.
3. Run the three vitest commands `@clawsweeper` specified as acceptance criteria:
   - `vitest run src/gateway/server-methods/agent-wait-dedupe.test.ts`
   - `vitest run src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts -t "agent.wait"`
   - `vitest run src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts -t "agent.wait"`
4. Capture live `respond(...)` payloads from `agentHandlers["agent.wait"]` against seeded `context.dedupe` representing real terminal-snapshot states (cached fast path, P1 isolation, partial metadata).

### Expected

`agent.wait` response includes `meta.agentMeta` with usage, cost, provider, and model metadata when the embedded runner result contains provider usage data. The metadata follows the SELECTED snapshot source: same-runId chat collisions must NOT leak agent telemetry; lifecycle-first races must carry the same `meta.agentMeta` shape as the dedupe-write race; partial `agentMeta` (e.g. provider/model only) is surfaced as-is.

### Actual

All three behaviours confirmed on HEAD `2cba15d4`:

- Cached fast path: `meta.agentMeta` present with `usage.inputTokens`, `usage.outputTokens`, `usage.cachedInputTokens`, `costUsd`, `provider`, `model`.
- P1 isolation: `meta` ABSENT from chat-active wait despite a rigged agent dedupe entry carrying `9999/9999/9999/0.99` telemetry — no leak.
- Partial metadata: provider/model surfaced as-is; no synthetic zero-fill for missing `usage` or `costUsd`.

49 vitest assertions passed across the three named acceptance-criteria files (24 + 7 + 3 from new tests, the rest from existing unchanged tests). 0 failures.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

```text
══ [A] agent.wait response (handler at HEAD 2cba15d4) — cached fast path ══
{
  "runId": "proof-cached-fast-path",
  "status": "ok",
  "startedAt": 1716653998211,
  "endedAt": 1716654001840,
  "meta": {
    "agentMeta": {
      "usage": { "inputTokens": 4521, "outputTokens": 834, "cachedInputTokens": 212 },
      "costUsd": 0.0873,
      "provider": "anthropic",
      "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6"
    }
  }
}

══ [B] agent.wait response (chat-active, agent meta IGNORED — no leak) ══
{
  "runId": "proof-p1-no-leak",
  "status": "ok",
  "startedAt": 1716654001000,
  "endedAt": 1716654004000
}

══ [C] agent.wait response (partial agentMeta — provider/model only) ══
{
  "runId": "proof-partial-meta",
  "status": "ok",
  "startedAt": 1716653998000,
  "endedAt": 1716653999500,
  "meta": {
    "agentMeta": {
      "provider": "deepseek",
      "model": "deepseek-chat"
    }
  }
}

✓ |gateway-client|  [A] cached-snapshot fast path: dedupe entry with agentMeta            67ms
✓ |gateway-client|  [B] P1 isolation: chat.send active, agent meta MUST NOT leak           2ms
✓ |gateway-client|  [C] partial agentMeta: provider/model only                             1ms
✓ |gateway-methods| [A] cached-snapshot fast path: dedupe entry with agentMeta            64ms
✓ |gateway-methods| [B] P1 isolation: chat.send active, agent meta MUST NOT leak           2ms
✓ |gateway-methods| [C] partial agentMeta: provider/model only                             1ms

Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests       6 passed (6)
Duration    48.57s
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified, not just CI, and how:

- Verified scenarios (HEAD `2cba15d4`, captured 2026-05-25):
  - Cloned fork branch at HEAD `2cba15d4` (auto-merge of upstream main on top of `f091512a`) and reset local working tree.
  - Ran `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` clean.
  - Ran all three vitest commands `@clawsweeper` named as acceptance criteria; all 49 assertions across the named files pass.
  - Invoked `agentHandlers["agent.wait"]` directly against seeded `context.dedupe` representing real terminal-snapshot states; captured live `respond(...)` payloads for three scenarios (cached fast path, P1 isolation, partial metadata).
  - Confirmed `meta.agentMeta` shape in responses matches the Paperclip-side adapter parser at `packages/adapters/openclaw-gateway/src/server/execute.ts:1443-1446`.
  - Confirmed P1 isolation: rigged `9999/9999/9999/0.99` agent telemetry does NOT leak when chat.send is active for the same runId.
  - Confirmed P2 determinism: the lifecycle-first race integration test (`agent.wait surfaces meta.agentMeta from lifecycle-first race`) carries the same `meta.agentMeta` shape via the lifecycle `end` event payload through `createSnapshotFromLifecycleEvent`.

- Edge cases checked:
  - Complete `agentMeta` with all four fields.
  - Partial usage token data.
  - Deepseek provider/model values.
  - Empty usage object.
  - Zero cost.
  - Missing provider/model.
  - Null or missing `agentMeta`.
  - Malformed usage value (number instead of object).
  - Same-runId chat collision (chat-fresher freshness rule).
  - Same-runId chat collision (chat-active `ignoreAgentTerminalSnapshot` rule).
  - Lifecycle-first race wins (P2).

- What you did **not** verify:
  - Full end-to-end Paperclip adapter ingestion against a live Paperclip instance.
  - Fleet-scale satellite upgrade rollout (parent HIVA-367 scope).
  - Historical cost backfill for pre-existing runs (out of scope).
  - End-to-end live gateway boot capturing the full WS round trip (the integration test in `server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts` runs a real `ws://127.0.0.1:${port}` gateway and passes; that exercise is the closest E2E coverage that fits in unit-runtime).
  - Runs that abort before the provider returns any usage data (partially covered by the parser's null/empty/malformed test branches).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Downstream clients may not yet consume the new metadata.
  - Mitigation: The change is additive under `meta.agentMeta`; existing clients can safely ignore it.

- Risk: Cost estimates could diverge if provider/model pricing config is incomplete.
  - Mitigation: The implementation uses the existing `estimateUsageCost` and `resolveModelCostConfig` pipeline rather than introducing a separate pricing path.

target.title: Fix plugin packaging recovery hints
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?
- Fixes the remaining #80490 misdirection where plugin packaging failures could still lead startup/config validation users toward the generic `openclaw doctor --fix` recovery hint.

Why does this matter now?
- ClawSweeper found that installed source-only TypeScript plugin packages should remain blocked unless publishers ship compiled JavaScript, but the recovery hint still implied a local doctor repair.

What is the intended outcome?
- Plugin packaging / compiled-runtime-output failures keep the packaging diagnostic and show update/reinstall-after-publisher-fix or disable/uninstall guidance instead of `openclaw doctor --fix`.
- Normal invalid config failures still show the doctor recovery hint.

What is intentionally out of scope?
- No doctor auto-build path for arbitrary third-party plugin source.
- No plugin build-system changes and no changelog edits.

What does success look like?
- Users with a source-only installed plugin package see packaging-specific remediation in config validation and gateway startup, without losing doctor guidance for unrelated invalid config cases.

What should reviewers focus on?
- The classifier in `src/config/recovery-policy.ts`, especially that it only suppresses doctor guidance for compiled-runtime-output packaging diagnostics and same-plugin `plugin not found` fallout.

<details>
<summary>Summary guidance</summary>

This PR description is the contributor's durable explanation of the change. Write it for human maintainers first; ClawSweeper and Barnacle use the same text to understand intent, proof, risk, and current review state.

Describe the intent and outcome in 2-5 bullets. Avoid restating the diff; reviewers and bots can read the changed files.

If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(<plugin-id>): beta blocker - <summary>` and link the matching `Beta blocker: <plugin-name> - <summary>` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation.

</details>

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #80490

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related: ClawSweeper guidance in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/80490#issuecomment-4416918935

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

ClawSweeper guidance requested a focused repair that corrects startup/config guidance without auto-building third-party plugin source.

<details>
<summary>Linked context guidance</summary>

Link the issue, PR, discussion, maintainer request, or owner request that explains why this PR should exist. Maintainer context helps reviewers and automation distinguish intended work from drive-by churn.

</details>

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: source-only installed plugin package referenced from config should fail with plugin packaging guidance, not `openclaw doctor --fix`.
- Real environment tested: Crabbox local-container Linux proof, disposable Docker image `openclaw-issue-80490-proof:local`, provider `local-container`, lease `cbx_0b5f611fb5a6`, slug `silver-shrimp`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: created a temporary Linux state dir with an installed `@openclaw/source-only-pack` package whose manifest points at `./index.ts` and no compiled JS, wrote config with `plugins.slots.memory="source-only-pack"`, then ran `pnpm openclaw config validate` and `timeout 30s pnpm openclaw gateway run`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
CONFIG_STATUS=1
OpenClaw config is invalid: /tmp/openclaw-issue-80490-state/openclaw.json
  × plugins.slots.memory: plugin not found: source-only-pack

This is a plugin packaging issue, not a local config problem.
Update or reinstall the plugin after the publisher ships compiled JavaScript, or disable/uninstall the plugin until then.
Inspect with openclaw config validate.

GATEWAY_STATUS=1
Gateway failed to start: Invalid config at /tmp/openclaw-issue-80490-state/openclaw.json.
plugins.slots.memory: plugin not found: source-only-pack
This is a plugin packaging issue, not a local config problem.
Update or reinstall the plugin after the publisher ships compiled JavaScript, or disable/uninstall the plugin until then.. Run openclaw gateway status --deep for diagnostics.
REAL_PROOF_OK issue-80490 plugin packaging startup/config validation hints
```

- Observed result after fix: both real CLI entrypoints failed for the source-only installed plugin package with packaging-specific remediation; grep checks confirmed `openclaw doctor --fix` was absent from both outputs.
- What was not tested: live VPS, live channels, provider accounts, or real third-party plugin registry state.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the normal Crabbox local-container sync path failed on this Windows host because rsync was unavailable in the sync shell, and AWS Crabbox was blocked by missing AWS credentials. I used Crabbox local-container with `--no-sync` and a disposable Docker image containing the patched checkout to keep the proof in a real Linux/container lane without touching live runtime state.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): ClawSweeper source-level repro in #80490 showed gateway startup still appended `formatInvalidConfigRecoveryHint()` for this failure shape before the patch.

<details>
<summary>Real behavior proof guidance</summary>

External contributors must show after-fix evidence from a real OpenClaw setup. Unit tests, mocks, lint, typechecks, snapshots, and CI are supplemental only.

Screenshots are encouraged even for CLI, console, text, or log changes. Terminal screenshots, copied live output, redacted runtime logs, recordings, and linked artifacts count.

If your environment cannot produce the ideal proof, explain that under `Proof limitations or environment constraints` so reviewers and ClawSweeper can direct the next step properly.

Be mindful of private information like IP addresses, API keys, phone numbers, non-public endpoints, or other private details when providing evidence.

</details>

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-startup-config.recovery.test.ts src/commands/config-validation.test.ts src/cli/config-cli.test.ts src/plugins/discovery.test.ts src/config/recovery-policy.test.ts
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/cli/config-cli.ts src/cli/config-cli.test.ts src/cli/config-recovery-hints.ts src/commands/config-validation.ts src/commands/config-validation.test.ts src/config/recovery-policy.ts src/config/recovery-policy.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-config.ts src/gateway/server-startup-config.recovery.test.ts src/plugins/discovery.test.ts
git diff --check
node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/cli/config-cli.ts src/cli/config-cli.test.ts src/cli/config-recovery-hints.ts src/commands/config-validation.ts src/commands/config-validation.test.ts src/config/recovery-policy.ts src/config/recovery-policy.test.ts src/gateway/server-startup-config.ts src/gateway/server-startup-config.recovery.test.ts src/plugins/discovery.test.ts
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.issue-80490.rebased.tsbuildinfo
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/test-src.issue-80490.rebased.tsbuildinfo
codex review --uncommitted
```

What regression coverage was added or updated?
- Added recovery-policy coverage for plugin packaging snapshots, mixed core invalidity, and unrelated missing-plugin cases.
- Added/updated config validation, config CLI, gateway startup recovery, and plugin discovery tests proving packaging guidance remains while `openclaw doctor --fix` is absent for source-only installed plugin packaging failures.
- Kept normal invalid config coverage proving the doctor hint remains for ordinary invalid configs.

What failed before this fix, if known?
- Gateway startup and config validation recovery paths could still append or print `openclaw doctor --fix` for this packaging failure shape.

If no test was added, why not?
- Tests were added and updated.

<details>
<summary>Testing guidance</summary>

List focused commands, not every incidental check. CI is useful support, but external PRs still need real behavior proof above when behavior changes.

</details>

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No config schema or migration behavior changed; only recovery guidance selection changed.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

Over-suppressing the generic doctor hint for mixed or unrelated invalid configs.

How is that risk mitigated?

The classifier requires a compiled-runtime-output plugin packaging diagnostic and only permits same-plugin `plugin not found` fallout; tests cover mixed core invalidity and unrelated missing plugins retaining doctor guidance.

<details>
<summary>Risk guidance</summary>

Use this for author judgment that is not obvious from the diff. ClawSweeper can see touched files, but it cannot know which behavior you think is risky, why the risk is acceptable, or what mitigation reviewers should verify.

</details>

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Review and CI.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

CI is pending after PR creation; external proof is included above.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

Addresses ClawSweeper guidance on #80490 by correcting diagnostics/recovery hints rather than auto-building third-party plugin source.

Keep this as the durable state for review progress. If useful information appears in comments, fold the current next action or blocker back here so maintainers and ClawSweeper do not need to reconstruct state from comment history.

target.title: fix(usage-cost): surface unpriced-model spend as missingCostEntries instead of a confident $0
target.body:
## Summary

- Treat token-burning usage with all-zero or unknown model pricing as missing cost instead of a confident complete $0.
- Preserve positive recorded transport costs and existing tiered-pricing recomputation.
- Bump the durable usage-cost cache version so old complete-$0 summaries are rebuilt.

Fixes #85858.

## Verification

- `CI=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=120000 fnm exec --using v24.15.0 -- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/infra/session-cost-usage.test.ts --reporter=dot --pool=forks --no-file-parallelism`
- Autoreview clean after the zero-rate pricing fixup.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `usage-cost` could report token-burning unpriced model usage as complete `$0` with `missingCostEntries: 0`, hiding real spend from budget and spike monitoring.

Real environment tested: live OpenClaw `2026.5.20` host with default `openai/gpt-5.5` API-key usage, plus focused regression tests on the rebased branch.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: applied the equivalent change to the live host and ran `openclaw gateway usage-cost --days 2 --json --expect-final` over the same logged usage; locally reran `src/infra/session-cost-usage.test.ts` after the maintainer fixup.

Evidence after fix: the same 1,780,235 tokens that previously reported `missingCostEntries: 0` now report `missingCostEntries: 32`; local regression suite passes 47 tests.

Observed result after fix: OpenClaw no longer treats all-zero unknown pricing as complete zero spend for token-burning turns. Positive recorded costs and tiered pricing remain intact.

What was not tested: macOS/Control UI cost views; a future explicit config contract for intentionally free zero-rate models.

target.title: feat(codex): diagnose native thread lifecycle
target.body:
## Summary

This is PR #3 in the Codex native-thread reuse stack, after #85978 and #86069. It does **not** change the 70k default guard, and it does **not** preserve bindings across context-engine compaction or successor transcript rollover yet. It makes Codex app-server native thread reuse, bypass, rotation, and compaction-invalidation decisions observable first.

Changes:

- Add `codex.native_thread.lifecycle` as a trusted diagnostic event.
- Add `extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-thread-diagnostics.ts` with stable lifecycle reasons and one helper for trusted events plus low-cardinality logs.
- Route native token/byte guard rotation, `thread_bootstrap` semantic reuse, context-engine/projection/dynamic-tool/MCP/environment/plugin/auth mismatch, native-tool-surface-disabled bypass, missing binding, app-server rejection, invalid image rejection, and context-engine-owned compaction invalidation through the helper.
- Keep #85978/#86069 behavior intact: matching active `thread_bootstrap` bindings still bypass proactive token/byte guards; legacy/non-bootstrap bindings still honor configured token/byte guards.
- Document the difference between model/app-server context overflow, OpenClaw assembly/precheck pressure, and native-thread reuse rotation.

## Why this matters

The confusing part of the slowdown is that the `70000` guard is not GPT-5.5's model context limit. It is a local warm-thread reuse threshold. If a legacy Codex native transcript is already above that threshold, OpenClaw refuses to reuse the saved native thread and starts a fresh app-server thread. That can make every turn pay cold-start context rebuild pressure even when the channel/session itself is fine.

PR #82981 is the historical source for the default: it introduced a guard that rotates stale Codex app-server bindings before resume when the bound native rollout is at or above 70k tokens, because large native Codex rollouts could keep getting resumed after OpenClaw had already compacted/mirrored a smaller transcript. So the default should be read as a proactive warm-thread reuse guard, not as a model-window cap, not as a bootstrap-size cap, and not as a carefully documented LCM reserve.

Example failure mode:

| Turn | Legacy/native transcript | Guard | Decision | Cost shape |
| --- | ---: | ---: | --- | --- |
| 1 | 86k | 70k | `native-token-guard` rotates | cold `thread/start`; bootstrap/context is rebuilt |
| 2 | still over guard | 70k | rotates again | cold path again |
| 3-10 | still over guard | 70k | repeats | no durable warm Codex thread |

With #86069 config support, `maxActiveTranscriptTokens: "120k"` preserves an 86k legacy binding, while `"50k"` rotates a 60k binding. Setting the token guard to `0` disables only proactive token rotation; byte guards and semantic identity mismatches can still rotate.

The best-case long-running context-engine path is different: `thread_bootstrap` projects the large context once into the fresh Codex thread, then reuses it as long as the context-engine id, policy fingerprint, projection epoch/fingerprint, and dynamic-tool surface still match. This PR emits `thread-bootstrap-semantic-reuse` only after that semantic match is proven.

```mermaid
graph TD
  A[OpenClaw turn] --> B{Saved Codex binding?}
  B -->|no| S[Start fresh native thread]
  B -->|yes| C{Semantic identity matches?}
  C -->|no| R[Rotate with mismatch reason]
  C -->|yes legacy/per-turn| G{Native token/byte guard exceeded?}
  G -->|yes| T[Rotate with native-token/native-byte reason]
  G -->|no| U[Resume warm native thread]
  C -->|yes thread_bootstrap| H[Emit thread-bootstrap-semantic-reuse]
  H --> U
  R --> S
  T --> S
```

## Adversarial review fixes in latest head

A four-agent adversarial pass found diagnostics issues rather than behavior changes in the guard itself. Latest head fixes them:

- Avoid false `thread-bootstrap-semantic-reuse` events from the early proactive guard bypass; semantic reuse is emitted only after the later identity match.
- Keep scoped ids/fingerprints in the trusted internal diagnostic event, but remove them from generic log payloads to avoid support-log/OTEL cardinality and privacy problems.
- Add `native-tool-surface-disabled` so transient native-disabled turns are not misclassified as `environment-selection-mismatch`.
- Preserve real `thread_bootstrap`/`per_turn` binding mode and context-engine metadata when native compaction rejects a stale binding.
- During overflow retry after session rollover, report the active successor session id and read the successor binding before logging/clearing it, so diagnostics name the actual successor thread only when one exists.

## Diagnostics

`codex.native_thread.lifecycle` includes stable reason/action fields plus thread/session/run ids, binding mode, context-engine identity, projection epoch/fingerprint, previous/current fingerprints, token/byte guard values, native/session token counts, prompt/developer-instruction size estimates, and bootstrap/context-engine contribution booleans.

It intentionally does not include raw prompt text, bootstrap file contents, tool args, or secrets. The companion generic log record is lower-cardinality and omits scoped thread/session ids, epoch/fingerprint fields, and path-like/error `extra` values.

## Non-goals

- No default guard change from `70000` in this PR.
- No migration or config rename.
- No binding preservation across context-engine compaction or successor transcript rollover yet.
- No change to PI runtime compaction semantics.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Codex native-thread lifecycle decisions now emit a structured trusted diagnostic event so an operator can distinguish native-token guard rotation from semantic reuse and other rotation families.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw source checkout on the Lexar worktree at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/worktrees/openclaw-codex-semantic-reuse-guard`, using the real OpenClaw package runtime with `node --import tsx`.
- **Exact steps or command run after fix**: Executed the real diagnostic helper through the package export path and listened through the real internal diagnostic runtime:

```bash
node --import tsx - <<'NODE'
import { onInternalDiagnosticEvent, resetDiagnosticEventsForTest, waitForDiagnosticEventsDrained } from 'openclaw/plugin-sdk/diagnostic-runtime';
import { CodexNativeThreadLifecycleReason, emitCodexNativeThreadLifecycleDiagnostic } from './extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-thread-diagnostics.ts';

resetDiagnosticEventsForTest();
const events = [];
const unsubscribe = onInternalDiagnosticEvent((event) => {
  if (event.type === 'codex.native_thread.lifecycle') events.push(event);
});
emitCodexNativeThreadLifecycleDiagnostic({
  action: 'rotated',
  reason: CodexNativeThreadLifecycleReason.NativeTokenGuard,
  threadId: 'thread-proof',
  sessionId: 'session-proof',
  sessionKey: 'agent:main:session-proof',
  bindingMode: 'legacy',
  maxActiveTranscriptTokens: 70000,
  nativeTokens: 86000,
  sessionTokens: 86000,
});
await waitForDiagnosticEventsDrained();
unsubscribe();
console.log(JSON.stringify(events[0], null, 2));
NODE
```

- **After-fix evidence**: Copied terminal output from that command:

```text
[agent/embedded] codex app-server native thread lifecycle: rotated (native-token-guard)
{
  "type": "codex.native_thread.lifecycle",
  "action": "rotated",
  "reason": "native-token-guard",
  "threadId": "thread-proof",
  "sessionId": "session-proof",
  "sessionKey": "agent:main:session-proof",
  "bindingMode": "legacy",
  "maxActiveTranscriptTokens": 70000,
  "nativeTokens": 86000,
  "sessionTokens": 86000,
  "seq": 2,
  "ts": 1779637794109
}
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The runtime emitted `codex.native_thread.lifecycle` with `action: "rotated"`, `reason: "native-token-guard"`, token guard values, native/session token counts, and thread/session identity, with no raw prompt text, bootstrap file contents, tool args, or secrets.
- **Latest-head validation stance**: After the adversarial-review amend, local Vitest/tsgo were intentionally not rerun on Codex Desktop; GitHub CI is the validation gate for this head. I ran only `git diff --check` locally.
- **What was not tested**: I did not run a live Discord gateway session through Codex app-server in this PR. That belongs to the next operational validation pass after this diagnostic surface lands.

## Test Plan

Tests added/extended for the latest head:

- token guard, byte guard, `120k` preserving 86k, `50k` rotating 60k, `0` disabling proactive token rotation, byte guard pre-read behavior, and sanitized lifecycle log payloads in `run-attempt.test.ts`
- matching `thread_bootstrap` semantic reuse, no false semantic reuse on mismatched over-budget bindings, projection/context-engine mismatch, and overflow rollover successor-binding diagnostics in `run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts`
- missing binding, stale binding, stale `thread_bootstrap` binding metadata, auth-profile mismatch, and context-engine-owned compaction invalidation in `compact.test.ts`
- dynamic-tool, MCP, plugin app, environment selection, native-tool-surface-disabled, and app-server resume rejection reason coverage through `startOrResumeThread`

Remote validation expected on GitHub CI for the pushed head `2372e215a2`.

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 3 official channel capability matrix
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR prevents the long tail from becoming a new pile of native branches. It expresses official app/API and regional plugin platforms as broker aliases and capability rows first, including WeChat/Weixin, and it enforces declared delivery capabilities before outbound handoff. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, payload/live-preview delivery requests, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 3 - Official/app platform capability matrix - Role: Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. - Head: `7bff662dee` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `7bff662dee` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 3 official/app channel capability matrix for Channel Broker. - Keeps the work at the broker contract/catalog layer: no native channel migration defaults change in this PR. - Stacks on Phase 2D Slack proof (#86157), Phase 2C Discord proof (#86156), Phase 2B Telegram proof (#86154), Phase 2A conformance baseline (#86153), and Phase 1 broker foundation (#86096). Closes #86113 Related #86105 Related #86108 Supersedes closed PR #86144, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. ## What changed - Adds canonical broker platform aliases while keeping `platform: string` open for provider-owned long-tail targets: - `teams` / `msteams` -> `microsoft-teams` - `googlechat` -> `google-chat` - `qq` -> `qqbot` - Aligns `channels.channel-broker.*.capabilities` config with the SDK capability shape: - `delivery` - `live` - `receive` - `native` - Adds focused proof for Microsoft Teams, Google Chat, Matrix, LINE, Feishu, QQ bot, Zalo, Mattermost, Nextcloud Talk, Twitch, IRC, Nostr, Tlon, and Synology Chat. - Documents the Phase 3 platform matrix and SDK capability shape. Focused diff for this stack layer: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Official/app channel aliases normalize through the broker catalog, and Phase 3 platforms route through provider-owned broker requests without enabling native migration defaults. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:teams:19:meeting-channel"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "teams-alias", "messageId": "native-teams-alias", "platform": "microsoft-teams", "conversation": { "id": "19:meeting-channel", "type": "channel" }, "requirements": { "text": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path canonicalized the Teams alias to `microsoft-teams` and produced a provider-owned broker request with no platform-specific native extension branch. - **What was not tested:** Live Teams, Google Chat, Matrix, regional, or self-hosted provider traffic; this PR proves the broker catalog/schema/conformance layer and leaves broad validation to GitHub Actions. ## Validation Run locally from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/channel-broker-vitest-include.json pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - `pnpm tsgo:core:test` - `git diff --check` - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/accounts.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/platforms.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md` - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - `node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md` - `pnpm dlx --config.resolution-mode=highest markdownlint-cli2 --config config/markdownlint-cli2.jsonc docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md` Note: `pnpm exec markdownlint-cli2 ...` was unavailable in the local workspace, so the documented `pnpm dlx` path was used. target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 2A conformance harness
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR is the regression shield. It turns the recurring channel pain into shared conformance: signed inbound webhooks, normalization, dedupe, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, preview/progress gates, and /verbose channelData. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, payload/live-preview delivery requests, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app and regional platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, WeChat/Weixin coverage, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers, including BlueBubbles-backed Mac/Messages constraints, without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2A - Conformance and signed inbound baseline - Role: Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff, payload/live-preview delivery requests, /verbose channelData preservation, and explicit listed-provider gating. - Head: `3f295c3742` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `3f295c3742` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2A Channel Broker conformance baseline on top of #86096. - Extends `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker` with inbound normalization, dedupe-key, provider capability normalization, and platform capability checks. - Adds bundled broker conformance coverage for durable inbound receive, durable final send receipts, retryable receipt failures, preview/progress capability gates, and `/verbose`/tool-message `channelData`. ## Rollout Context Closes #86109 Related #86108 Related #86105 Stacked on #86096 Supersedes closed PR #86133, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. This PR intentionally does not migrate Telegram, Discord, or Slack routing. Native defaults stay unchanged until the Phase 2B/2C/2D proofs land behind opt-in broker routing. For review, the focused stack diff is: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** The broker conformance baseline normalizes inbound events, builds durable dedupe keys, evaluates preview/progress capabilities, and sends outbound requests through the bundled broker lifecycle without platform-specific core branches. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that imported `src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts`, created a Telegram inbound event, built the durable dedupe key, evaluated Discord preview/progress capabilities, and sent broker outbound requests through `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text`. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "inboundDedupeKey": "acme:bot-main:telegram:live-proof-telegram-1", "previewProgressSupported": true } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live SDK/plugin path produced the expected durable inbound key and capability decision outside the Vitest harness, proving the Phase 2A contract surface can carry the normalized lifecycle facts that later channel migrations consume. - **What was not tested:** Live provider network traffic and the full local suite; those remain covered by later provider proofs and GitHub Actions. ### Behavior The broker contract now has a conformance harness for the lifecycle paths that regress most often across maintained chat channels: inbound normalization/dedupe, durable final sends, provider receipts, retry/failure propagation, preview/progress capability checks, and `/verbose` tool-message metadata. ### Environment - Local checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker` - Branch: `feat/channel-broker-conformance` - Head: `3f295c3742` (current Phase 2A head) ### Steps 1. Normalized a synthetic Telegram inbound event through the SDK and accepted it through the durable inbound receive journal. 2. Replayed the same dedupe key while pending and after completion to prove duplicate/redelivery handling. 3. Sent a synthetic Slack final message through the bundled broker runtime and verified provider receipt commit material. 4. Returned a retryable Discord provider receipt and verified the send fails with structured receipt context. 5. Built preview/finalize broker requests and asserted Discord preview/progress support through declared capabilities. 6. Passed `/verbose` tool-message policy through broker `channelData` without platform-specific branching. ### Evidence - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/channel-broker-vitest-include.json pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - 1 file passed, 7 tests passed. - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - 3 files passed, 10 tests passed. - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - passed. - `pnpm tsgo:core:test` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/outbound.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - passed. - `git diff --check` - passed. ### Observed Result The SDK helpers normalize provider payloads without dropping native metadata, the bundled plugin refuses non-sent provider receipts as durable-send failures, and conformance tests prove the current broker path can represent the Phase 2 lifecycle requirements without changing user-visible channel defaults. ### Not Tested - Full local suite. Per `/Volumes/LEXAR` policy, broad validation is left to GitHub Actions. - Live Telegram, Discord, or Slack provider traffic. Those are intentionally scoped to Phase 2B/2C/2D. ## Current Stack Gate Context This branch is rebased on the current Phase 1 foundation head. Focused local validation is documented above or below; broad validation remains on GitHub Actions.

GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2C - Discord opt-in broker routing proof - Role: Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. - Head: `56bc4818a1` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `56bc4818a1` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json --noEmit` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2C Discord broker routing proof on top of #86154, #86153, and #86096. - Normalizes broker-routed Discord DMs and channel threads without importing native Discord extension internals. - Preserves non-channel conversation type in canonical broker routes so Discord DMs remain direct routes. ## Rollout Context Closes #86111 Related #86108 Related #86105 Stacked on #86154 Stacked on #86153 Stacked on #86096 Supersedes closed PR #86139, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. Native Discord defaults stay unchanged. Existing prefix ownership still keeps `discord:` owned by the native Discord plugin when installed; this PR proves the opt-in broker route through `broker:discord:...` and the broker alias path when native Discord is absent. For review, the focused stack diff is: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Discord DM and threaded-channel targets can route through `channel-broker` while preserving direct/channel conversation semantics and provider-owned ids. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:discord:user:123456789012345678"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "discord-dm", "messageId": "native-discord-dm", "platform": "discord", "conversation": { "id": "123456789012345678", "type": "direct" }, "requirements": { "text": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path produced a direct Discord broker provider request rather than an ambiguous channel-looking target. - **What was not tested:** Live Discord API traffic and the full local suite; this PR stays scoped to opt-in broker routing proof. ### Behavior OpenClaw can represent Discord DMs and channel threads through `channel-broker` using stable broker semantics: provider platform `discord`, direct/channel conversation type, conversation id, and optional thread id. ### Environment - Local checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker` - Branch: `feat/channel-broker-discord-routing` - Head: `56bc4818a1` (current Discord proof head) ### Steps 1. Sent a synthetic message to `broker:discord:user:123456789012345678`. 2. Verified the provider request used `platform: "discord"`, `conversation.type: "direct"`, and `conversation.id: "123456789012345678"`. 3. Sent a synthetic message to `broker:discord:channel:222222222222222222?threadId=333333333333333333`. 4. Verified the provider request used channel conversation id plus `threadId`. 5. Resolved the outbound session route for the Discord DM target. 6. Verified the route stayed direct and canonicalized to `discord:direct%3A123456789012345678`. ### Evidence - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - 2 files passed, 13 tests passed. - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - passed. - `git diff --check` - passed. ### Observed Result Discord DMs and threads now flow through broker target parsing and provider request semantics, not through core or native Discord extension branches. ### Not Tested - Live Discord API traffic; this PR is the opt-in broker migration proof, not a live provider connector. - Full local suite. Per `/Volumes/LEXAR` policy, broad validation is left to GitHub Actions. ## Current Stack Gate Context This branch is rebased on the current Phase 1 foundation head. Focused local validation is documented above or below; broad validation remains on GitHub Actions.
